From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Canadian playwright and actor (born 1974)

| Trey Anthony | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | (1974-02-18) February 18, 1974 (age 52)  London, England |
| Occupation | Playwright, actor, producer |
| Genre | Comedy |
| Notable works | *[Da Kink in My Hair](/wiki/Da_Kink_in_My_Hair "Da Kink in My Hair")* |
| Notable awards | NAACP Theatre Award (2007), Gemini Award (2008) |
| Website | |
| [Trey Anthony](https://www.treyanthony.com) | |

**Trey Anthony** (born February 18, 1974) is a British-born Canadian playwright, actor, and producer, best known for her award-winning play and television series *[Da Kink in My Hair](/wiki/Da_Kink_in_My_Hair_(TV_series) "Da Kink in My Hair (TV series)")*.[[1]](#cite_note-swartz-1) As a producer, she worked for the Women's Television Network and the Urban Women's Comedy Festival.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) She founded Trey Anthony Studios, a television and theatre production company dedicated to producing new works of theatre.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trey_Anthony&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Born in [London, England](/wiki/London,_England "London, England") to [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaicans "Jamaicans") parents, Anthony arrived in Canada at 12 years old with her mother. They lived in the working class district of [Rexdale](/wiki/Rexdale "Rexdale") in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") before moving to suburban [Brampton](/wiki/Brampton "Brampton"). Anthony's mother had left for Canada ahead of her, leaving her from the ages of 6 to 12 to be raised by her grandmother.

Anthony's grandmother had in turn left her mother in Jamaica when leaving for the UK.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Anthony's grandmother had been part of the Domestic Scheme Act, allowing her to go to a first world country if she proved she had no family ties.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

She has a brother, Darren Anthony, who is also a writer.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trey_Anthony&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

She is a regular on the Canadian comedy circuit. She began doing stand-up comedy during African Nubian Comedy Nights where she honed her comedic wit and timing.[[6]](#cite_note-thestar-6) She soon became a crowd favourite and began writing and producing her own sketch comedy shows at Second City. These shows sold out monthly and Anthony moved her monthly shows to a bigger venue, growing her audience, and creating more demand for her theatrical work and projects.

She is a recipient of the prestigious [Harry Jerome Award](/wiki/Harry_Jerome_Awards "Harry Jerome Awards") for the arts and the recipient of an Eve Ensler Award of the Arts.

In 2017, Anthony launched her new brand, *Black Girl in Love*, which features the first lifestyle planner/organizer geared at professional black woman and also includes merchandise, workshops and retreats.

She has performed at [The Second City](/wiki/The_Second_City "The Second City"), the Urban Womyn's Comedy Festival and Toronto's [St. Lawrence Centre for the Arts](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Centre_for_the_Arts "St. Lawrence Centre for the Arts"). She was also a writer and performer for [Kenny Robinson](/wiki/Kenny_Robinson_(comedian) "Kenny Robinson (comedian)")'s sketch comedy show *After Hours with Kenny Robinson* and a writer for *[The Chris Rock Show](/wiki/The_Chris_Rock_Show "The Chris Rock Show")*.[[6]](#cite_note-thestar-6)

In 2017, her play *How Black Mothers Say I Love You* debuted at the Factory Theatre, Toronto.[[7]](#cite_note-clay-7)

In 2020, Anthony appeared in [CBC Gem](/wiki/CBC_Gem "CBC Gem")'s *[Queer Pride Inside](/wiki/Queer_Pride_Inside "Queer Pride Inside")* special.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### 'Da Kink in My Hair

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trey_Anthony&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 'Da Kink in My Hair")]

Her hit play and television series, *['Da Kink in My Hair](/wiki/Da_Kink_in_My_Hair "Da Kink in My Hair")*, has received tremendous critical acclaim, and was named one of the top ten plays in Canadian theatrical history and the winner of four NAACP and received several [Dora Mavor Moore Awards](/wiki/Dora_Mavor_Moore_Awards "Dora Mavor Moore Awards"). Originally set as a one-woman show, *'Da Kink* documents the lives of women in a Caribbean style Jamaican hair salon in Toronto.[[9]](#cite_note-National_Arts_Centre-9)

Starting out at the Toronto French Festival in 2001[[9]](#cite_note-National_Arts_Centre-9) *'Da Kink* has been produced in California, New York, London, and was the first Canadian play to be produced at the [Princess of Wales Theatre](/wiki/Princess_of_Wales_Theatre "Princess of Wales Theatre").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

Anthony is the first Black Canadian woman to write and produce a television show on a major prime time Canadian network. A [television series version of '*Da Kink in My Hair*](/wiki/Da_Kink_in_My_Hair_(TV_series) "Da Kink in My Hair (TV series)") began airing in 2007 and Anthony was a cast member before the show was cancelled in 2009.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Trey Anthony Studios

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trey_Anthony&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Trey Anthony Studios")]

Trey Anthony Studios was founded by Anthony with the mission to "produce television and theater for urban audiences."[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) It has produced and continuous to produce Anthony's works and has also produced *Secrets of a Black Boy*, a play written by her brother Darren.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Selected works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trey_Anthony&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Selected works")]

* [*'Da Kink in my Hair*](/wiki/Da_Kink_in_My_Hair "Da Kink in My Hair")
* *I Am Not a Dinner Mint, The Crap Women Swallow to Stay in a Relationship!*
* *How Black Mothers Say I Love You*
* *Black Girl in Love (with Herself)*
* *Brampton Walk of fame*

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trey_Anthony&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Awards")]

* *Eve Ensler Vagina Warrior Award* 2005[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* *NAACP Theatre Award* 2007[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* *Gemini Award* 2008
* *Harry Jerome Award* 2009[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* *Egale Canada – Queering Black History Award* 2009[[14]](#cite_note-14)
